description.en-us: The base units of the International System of Units (SI), our best effort to upgrade historical units of measurement using fancy physical constants.
The base unit of length in the International System of Units (SI), defined as the length light travels in vacuum in 1 / 299,792,458 seconds.
The base unit of mass in the International System of Units (SI), defined by the vibrations of a cesium atom.
The base unit of time in the International System of Units (SI), defined by the vibrations of a cesium atom.
The Universe's favorite units of measurement. "Natural" units constructed from fundamental constants.
The "natural" unit of length, constructed from fundamental constants. Around this size, our ideas about space break down.
The "natural" unit of mass, constructed from fundamental constants. Current theories can't describe particles of this mass.
The "natural" unit of time, constructed from fundamental constants. Around this timescale, our ideas about time break down.
A subatomic particle that carries a negative charge and surrounds the atomic nucleus.
A positively charged subatomic particle that, along with the neutron, makes up the atomic nucleus.
An uncharged subatomic particle that helps bind protons together in the nucleus.
The nucleus of a deuterium atom. An isotope of hydrogen, it's just a single proton and neutron bound together by the strong force.
Mediated by W+, W-, and Z bosons, the weak nuclear interaction is responsible for the radioactive decay of neutrons.
Mediated by gluons, the strong nuclear interaction binds quarks within nucleons and nucleons within nuclei.
Lead-208 is the heaviest known stable nucleus, featuring a "doubly magic" number of protons and neutrons.
The record-holding gamma ray from space, detected by the Large High Altitude Air Shower Observatory.

Gamma rays are very high energy photons often produced by radioactive decay. X-rays are high energy photons, though not as high energy as gamma rays, often produced by the collisions of charged particles.
The fuzzy transition point where ultraviolet light becomes ionizing X-ray radiation.
The shortest wavelength of light that the human eye can perceive.
The longest wavelength of light that the human eye can perceive.
The boundary near 1 millimeter where thermal infrared heat transitions into microwave radiation.
The 1-meter mark where shorter microwaves are categorized as longer radio waves.
The high-frequency signals used by mobile networks to transmit data at high speeds over short ranges.
Lower frequency radio waves used by mobile phones for better range and penetration through obstacles.
The stretched-out light from the early universe that now permeates space as a microwave glow.
A theoretical limit set by the "plasma frequency" of ionized gas between stars.

displayName.en-us: Longest Radio Possible in Solar System

description.en-us: Radio waves so long they are reflected by the thin plasma filling our solar system.
The longest radio waves that can pass through Earth's atmosphere without being bounced back into space.
A famous ultraviolet spectral line used by astronomers to map the distribution of hydrogen across the cosmos.
The specific infrared frequency at which you are glowing right now due to your body heat.

displayName.en-us: IR Wavelength of Red Hot Metal

description.en-us: The infrared wavelength emitted by metal just before it begins to glow a visible red.
The 21-centimeter radio signal emitted by cold hydrogen that allows us to map the spiral arms of our galaxy.
The vast average distance between the sparse particles of plasma that fill the space between planets.
The average separation between gas and dust particles in the interstellar medium of our galaxy.
The immense distance between the extremely sparse hydrogen ions that make up the Intergalactic Medium, the stuff in the gaps between galaxies.

displayName.en-us: Between Particles in Best Artificial Vacuum

description.en-us: Even in the highest-quality vacuums created by humans, millions of molecules remain in every cubic centimeter.

displayName.en-us: Between Air Molecules at Sea Level

description.en-us: The average distance between molecules in the air you breathe is about 10 times their own size.
Particles in the Earth's solid inner core are packed tightly under pressures millions of times higher than at the surface.
Ions in the suns core are crushed so closely together that nuclear fusion can ignite.
The thin but incredibly hot plasma of the suns outer atmosphere has vastly more space between ions than the core.
The average distance between neighboring H2O molecules in liquid water at room temperature.
Neutrons in these collapsed stellar remnants are packed as tightly as they are inside an atomic nucleus.
A state of degenerate matter where ions are squeezed into a space far smaller than in any normal solid.
The typical distance between star systems in our galaxy is measured in light-years, making collisions extremely rare.
The colossal separation between the islands of stars we call galaxies.
Molecular hydrogen consists of just two protons and two electrons, making it the simplest, smallest, and lightest molecule.
The largest known molecule, a protein that acts as a spring in muscles. Humans have it, but ironically, mouse titin is even bigger.
A simple sugar that serves as the primary source of energy for most living organisms.
A polar molecule essential for all known forms of life, often described as the universal solvent.
The fundamental building blocks of proteins, which carry out most of the work inside your cells.
A sophisticated molecular machine that copies DNA with incredible speed and accuracy during cell division.
The most abundant protein in mammals, providing structural support to skin, bones, and connective tissues.
A single, long strand of DNA that is tightly coiled and packed into a structure visible under a microscope.
DNA that has condensed into compact, X-shaped structures to ensure safe transport into new cells.
The ultra-thin oily layer that protects the contents of a cell and controls what enters and exits.
Adeno-associated viruses are among the tiniest known, measuring only about 20 nanometers across.
A giant virus discovered in South America that possesses one of the most complex genetic toolkits of any virus.
The smallest known organisms capable of growing and reproducing without a host cell.
While technically a single cell, its massive size is due to the large amount of stored yolk.
The tiny male reproductive cell is specialized for one thing: swimming to an egg.
The female reproductive cell is large enough to be seen with the naked eye, about the size of a grain of sand.
The flat, protective cells that form the outermost barrier between your body and the environment.
The mobile defenders of your immune system that seek out and destroy invading pathogens.
Ancient bacteria-like structures that now live inside our cells to produce chemical energy.
The tiny protein factories found in all living cells, translating genetic code into physical traits.
The control center of the cell that houses the vast majority of our genetic information.
A single nerve fiber that can stretch from the base of your spine all the way down to your big toe.
Nerve fibers in the largest animal on Earth can reach lengths of over 25 meters.
The long, cable-like projections of neurons that carry electrical signals throughout your body.
The width of a strand of hair, often used as a standard reference for measuring micro-scale objects.
The size of the tiny electronic switches on modern computer chips, which can be just a few nanometers wide.
The upper limit of human hearing, roughly 20,000 Hz, which gradually declines as we age.
The deep, low-frequency rumble at 20 Hz that marks the bottom edge of human hearing.
Pigeons can detect incredibly low-frequency infrasound from distant storms or mountains.

displayName.en-us: Highest Sounds Animals Hear (Greater wax moths)

description.en-us: These moths have the highest recorded hearing range of any animal, reaching frequencies of 300,000 Hz.
The extreme limit of high-frequency sound waves generated in high-tech research laboratories.
High-frequency tremors that are often the first to be felt near the epicenter of an earthquake.
Massive low-frequency waves that can ripple through the entire planet during a powerful earthquake.
Ripples and swells on the ocean surface generated by the friction of wind blowing across the water.
The shorter-wavelength end of the massive sea waves triggered by underwater earthquakes or landslides.
Vast waves that can stretch over 100 kilometers from crest to crest as they race across the deep ocean.
Our home planet, the only place in the universe known to harbor life.
Earth's only natural satellite, which stabilizes our planets wobble and creates the tides.
The elliptical path the Moon takes around Earth, averaging about 384,400 kilometers away.
The massive star at the center of our solar system that provides the energy for life on Earth.
The average distance from the Earth to the Sun, approximately 150 million kilometers.
The fastest orbital path in the solar system, keeping the scorched planet closest to the Sun.
A vast path through the outer solar system taken by our solar systems largest planet.
The distant track taken by the ringed planet, nearly twice as far from the Sun as Jupiter.
A tilted, highly elliptical path that occasionally brings the dwarf planet closer to the Sun than Neptune.
A gas giant famous for its elaborate ring system and dozens of moons.
The edge of Saturn's spectacular main rings, stretching far beyond the planets surface.
A failed star that is too large to be a planet but too small to ignite nuclear fusion in its core.
Despite being thousands of kilometers wide, Saturn's rings are incredibly thin, averaging only about 10 meters.
A solid ball of iron and nickel at the center of the Earth, kept solid by immense pressure despite the heat.
A layer of liquid iron and nickel that generates Earth's protective magnetic field as it flows.
The thick layer of hot, solid-ish rock that makes up the bulk of Earth's volume.
The thick, lighter layer of rock that forms the continents we live on.
The thinner, denser layer of volcanic rock that forms the floor of the worlds oceans.
A dwarf planet in the asteroid belt that contains nearly a third of the belts total mass.
One of the smallest known asteroids, a tiny space rock only about 2 meters wide.
An incredibly dense neutron star that is among the smallest and most energetic stars known.
The collapsed core of a massive star, so dense that a teaspoon of its matter would weigh a billion tons.
A tiny, hot remnant of a dead star that is part of a unique binary system.
A type of star that is cooler and smaller than typical main-sequence stars of the same brightness.
An unusually large white dwarf located in a complex symbiotic star system.
Small, cool stars that are the most common type in the Milky Way and can live for trillions of years.
The most massive stars currently fusing hydrogen, pushing the physical limits of stellar stability.
The brightest star in Earth's night sky, also known as the Dog Star.
The vast size the Sun will reach as a red giant, likely swallowing the inner planets.
An extreme red hypergiant so large that it could fit nearly 5 billion Suns inside it.
The smallest known hydrogen-burning star, it lives in a triple star system.
The most massive and luminous star known, it lurks in the Large Magellanic Cloud.
Bright spheres of plasma in space, so large they are held together by gravity.
The outer edge of the icy region beyond Neptune where Pluto and many other dwarf planets reside.
The theoretical outer boundary of our solar system, home to trillions of dormant comets.
The beginning of the vast, spherical shell of icy objects that surrounds our solar system.
The boundary where the solar wind from our Sun is finally pushed back by the winds of other stars.
The current location of the furthest human-made object, now traveling through interstellar space.
The distance light travels in one year, approximately 9.46 trillion kilometers.
A unit of astronomical distance equal to about 3.26 light-years, used by professionals to map the stars.
The theoretical size of a black hole with the same mass as our Sun.
The distance to the closest star system to our own, roughly 4.24 light-years away.
The distance to the most distant individual star ever detected, seen as it was 12.9 billion years ago.
The distance from Earth to the supermassive black hole at the heart of the Milky Way.
Our home galaxy, a barred spiral containing hundreds of billions of stars.

displayName.en-us: To Magellanic Clouds from Galactic Center

description.en-us: The distance to the two satellite galaxies that orbit our Milky Way.
The distance to our neighbor galaxy, which is currently on a collision course with the Milky Way.
One of the largest known galaxies, a supergiant elliptical that dwarfs our Milky Way.
A dense, spherical collection of hundreds of thousands of ancient stars held together by gravity.
The expanding leftovers of a star that exploded in a violent supernova thousands of years ago.
The spherical region that contains all matter we can observe from Earth.
The farthest distance from which light emitted now will ever reach us. Objects farther than this can never affect us.
The small cluster of galaxies that includes the Milky Way, Andromeda, and about 50 other smaller galaxies.
The vast collection of galaxy groups that includes our own Local Group.
A gravitational anomaly in intergalactic space that is pulling our galaxy and others toward it.
The supermassive black hole at the center of our galaxy, weighing as much as 4 million Suns.
A massive black hole formed by the collision of two others, detected through ripples in spacetime.
The most massive black holes known, some weighing as much as 60 billion Suns.
The invisible cloud of dark matter that surrounds and outweighs the visible stars of our galaxy.
One of the largest structures in the universe, a thread-like bridge of galaxies stretching over a billion light-years.
Building blocks for our world of matter, neutral atoms consist of an equal number of protons and electrons, plus enough neutrons to bind protons together in the nucleus.
Hydrogen: The lightest and most abundant element in the universe, fueling the stars through nuclear fusion.
Helium: The second lightest element, first discovered in the Suns atmosphere before being found on Earth.
Lithium: A soft, silvery metal that is the lightest solid element and a critical component of modern batteries.
Beryllium: A lightweight, high-melting-point metal often used in aerospace and X-ray windows.
Boron: An essential nutrient for plants and a key ingredient in heat-resistant glassware.
Carbon: The versatile building block of life that forms the basis of all organic chemistry.
Nitrogen: The most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere, essential for protein and DNA.
Oxygen: The highly reactive gas that fuels cellular respiration and makes up about 21% of our air.
Fluorine: The most reactive of all chemical elements, commonly used to prevent tooth decay.
Neon: A noble gas famous for its vivid orange-red glow when used in advertising signs.
Sodium: A soft, silvery metal that reacts explosively with water and is a key part of table salt.
Magnesium: A lightweight metal that burns with a brilliant white light and is vital for photosynthesis.
Aluminium: The most abundant metal in Earth's crust, prized for its low density and corrosion resistance.
Silicon: The backbone of modern electronics and the primary component of sand and glass.
Phosphorus: A highly reactive nonmetal essential for energy transfer in cells and the structure of bones.
Sulfur: A bright yellow nonmetal often associated with the smell of rotten eggs and volcanic activity.
Chlorine: A greenish-yellow gas frequently used to disinfect swimming pools and drinking water.
Argon: The most common noble gas on Earth, often used to provide an inert atmosphere for welding.
Potassium: A soft metal that helps regulate fluid balance and nerve signals in the human body.
Calcium: A silvery-white metal that is the primary structural component of teeth, bones, and shells.
Scandium: A rare-earth metal used in high-strength aluminum alloys for sports equipment and aerospace.
Titanium: A strong, lightweight, and corrosion-resistant metal frequently used in médical implants.
Vanadium: A hard, silvery-grey metal primarily used as an additive to strengthen steel alloys.
Chromium: A shiny, hard metal that gives stainless steel its corrosion resistance and rubies their red color.
Manganese: An essential mineral for bone health and a key component in the production of steel.
Iron: The most common element on Earth by mass, forming the core of our planet and the basis of steel.
Cobalt: A magnetic metal used in high-strength alloys and as a blue pigment in ceramics and glass.
Nickel: A silvery-white metal used in coins, rechargeable batteries, and stainless steel.
Copper: A reddish-orange metal prized for its excellent electrical conductivity and use in plumbing.
Zinc: A bluish-white metal used to galvanize steel and as an essential micronutrient for the immune system.
Gallium: A unique metal that melts in your hand and is widely used in high-speed electronics.
Germanium: A semiconductor material that was used to create the first transistors.
Arsenic: A notorious poison that also has historic uses in pigments and modern uses in semiconductors.
Selenium: An essential trace element that also conducts electricity better when exposed to light.
Bromine: The only nonmetallic element that exists as a liquid at room temperature.
Krypton: A noble gas used alongside neon in high-efficiency fluorescent lamps and flashbulbs.
Rubidium: An extremely reactive soft metal that is used in high-precision atomic clocks.
Strontium: A metal that burns with a brilliant red flame, making it a staple in fireworks and flares.
Yttrium: A silvery-white metal used to create the red phosphors in old television screens.
Zirconium: A highly corrosion-resistant metal used in nuclear reactors and as a diamond-like gemstone.
Niobium: A soft, grey metal used in superconducting magnets and high-strength alloys for jet engines.
Molybdenum: A hard metal with a very high melting point, used to strengthen steel for extreme temperatures.
Technetium: The first element to be produced artificially, widely used as a radioactive tracer in medicine.
Ruthenium: A rare transition metal used to harden platinum and as a catalyst in chemical reactions.
Rhodium: One of the rarest and most valuable precious metals, used primarily in catalytic converters.
Palladium: A precious metal that can absorb 900 times its own volume in hydrogen gas.
Silver: The element with the highest electrical and thermal conductivity of any known metal.
Cadmium: A soft, bluish-white metal historically used in yellow pigments and rechargeable batteries.
Indium: A very soft metal used to create the transparent conductive coatings on touchscreens.
Tin: A silvery metal used for centuries to prevent corrosion in tin cans and as a component of bronze.
Antimony: A semi-metal that expands as it freezes, often used to harden lead alloys.
Tellurium: A rare semi-metal used to improve the machinability of steel and in solar panels.
Iodine: A lustrous purple-black solid that is essential for the production of thyroid hormones.
Xenon: A heavy noble gas used in specialized light sources like IMAX projectors and ion thrusters.
Caesium: The most reactive of all metals, used to define the standard length of a second.
Barium: A soft, silvery metal used in barium swallows to image the digestive system with X-rays.
Lanthanum: The first of the rare-earth elements, used in battery electrodes and high-quality camera lenses.
Cerium: The most abundant rare-earth metal, famous for creating sparks when struck in lighter flints.
Praseodymium: A soft, silvery metal that gives specialized glass a unique yellow tint.
Neodymium: A rare-earth metal used to create the strongest known permanent magnets.
Promethium: The only rare-earth element with no stable isotopes, often used in nuclear batteries.
Samarium: A hard, silvery metal used in high-temperature magnets and as a neutron absorber.
Europium: The most reactive rare-earth element, used to create the red and blue phosphors in displays.
Gadolinium: A rare-earth metal used as a contrast agent in MRI scans and in specialized nuclear applications.
Terbium: A silvery-grey metal used in the manufacture of electronic devices and high-efficiency lighting.
Dysprosium: A metal with high magnetic strength used in laser materials and nuclear reactor control rods.
Holmium: The element with the highest magnetic strength of any naturally occurring element.
Erbium: A pinkish metal used to amplify signals in long-distance fiber optic cables.
Thulium: The rarest naturally occurring rare-earth metal, used as a portable X-ray source.
Ytterbium: A soft, silvery metal used in high-intensity lasers and as a doping agent in fiber optics.
Lutetium: The densest and hardest of the lanthanide elements, used as a catalyst in petroleum cracking.
Hafnium: A corrosion-resistant metal with a high affinity for neutrons, used in nuclear control rods.
Tantalum: A highly corrosion-resistant metal used to make tiny, high-performance capacitors for cell phones.
Tungsten: The element with the highest melting point, traditionally used in incandescent light bulb filaments.
Rhenium: One of the rarest elements in Earth's crust, used in high-temperature superalloys for jet engines.
Osmium: The densest naturally occurring element, prized for its extreme hardness and durability.
Iridium: One of the most corrosion-resistant metals known, often associated with the asteroid that killed the dinosaurs.
Platinum: A dense, stable, and precious metal used in jewelry and as a vital industrial catalyst.
Gold: A noble metal valued for its beauty, rarity, and exceptional resistance to chemical corrosion.
Mercury: The only metal that exists as a liquid at room temperature, famous for its use in thermometers.
Thallium: A soft, grey metal that is highly toxic and was once widely used as a rodenticide.
Lead: A dense, soft metal that has been used for millennia but is now known to be a potent neurotoxin.
Bismuth: A brittle, silvery-pink metal used in stomach medicines and as a non-toxic replacement for lead.
Polonium: A highly radioactive and toxic element discovered by Marie Curie and named after her homeland.
Astatine: The rarest naturally occurring element on Earth, with less than one gram present in the entire crust at any time.
Radon: A colorless, odorless radioactive gas that can accumulate in buildings and pose a health risk.
Francium: The second rarest naturally occurring element, so unstable that it has no practical uses.
Radium: An intensely radioactive element once used in glow-in-the-dark watch dials before the health risks were known.
Actinium: A silvery radioactive metal that glows in the dark with an eerie blue light.
Thorium: A radioactive metal that is three times more abundant than uranium and a potential future nuclear fuel.
Protactinium: A rare, dense, and highly toxic radioactive metal found in uranium ores.
Uranium: The heaviest naturally occurring element found in significant quantities, vital for nuclear power and weapons.
Neptunium: The first transuranic element ever produced, formed in nuclear reactors from uranium.
Plutonium: A radioactive element used in nuclear weapons and as a long-term fuel source for space probes.
Americium: A synthetic, radioactive element used in most household smoke detectors to detect smoke particles.
Curium: A synthetic, radioactive element used to make the Pu-238 for powering old pacemakers. Its most stable isotope is Cm-247, with 96 protons and 151 neutrons.
Berkelium
Californium
Einsteinium
Fermium
Mendelevium
Nobelium
Lawrencium
Rutherfordium
Dubnium
Seaborgium
Bohrium
Hassium
Meitnerium
Darmstadtium
Roentgenium
Copernicium
Nihonium
Flerovium
Moscovium
Livermorium
Tennessine

Oganesson: The synthetic, radioactive element with the highest atomic number and atomic mass of all known elements. Relativistic effects mean it may be a solid at room temperature rather than a noble gas.
The fundamental particle discovered at the Large Hadron Collider that gives mass to other fundamental particles through its interaction with the Higgs field.
A "heavy electron" that is about 200 times more massive and decays in just a few microseconds.
A span of 3,600 seconds, originally based on dividing the day into 24 equal parts.
The time it takes for Earth to complete one full orbit around the Sun.
The estimated age of the universe, currently calculated to be roughly 13.8 billion years.
